original_hypothesis.title: Swapped Return Values in _calculateRepaySize Break All Order Fills After Interest Accrual
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The _calculateRepaySize function returns (uint repayAmt, uint repayShares) but _calculateFillOrderInfo destructures the return as (repayShares, repayAmt), swapping debt shares and token amounts. After any interest accrues (shares ≠ amounts), fillOrder attempts to repay using the token amount as share count (too high), causing reverts. This creates a permanent DoS on all order fills for pools with accrued interest.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: _calculateRepaySize declares returns as (uint repayAmt, uint repayShares). The call site assigns to (repayShares, repayAmt) = _calculateRepaySize(...). Named returns follow declaration order, so position 0 (repayAmt) maps to caller's repayShares and position 1 (repayShares) maps to caller's repayAmt — completely reversed.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _calculateRepaySize returns `(uint repayAmt, uint repayShares)` at lines 413-425; call site at line 377 destructures as `(repayShares, repayAmt) = _calculateRepaySize(...)` — positions are swapped

preconditions: Any lending pool has accrued interest (debtShareToAmt ratio > 1:1)
At least one active margin order exists
Order trigger price conditions are met

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Return value destructuring mismatch between function declaration and call site
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: repayShares gets token amount, repayAmt gets share count; fillOrder uses repayAmt (shares) for safeTransferFrom and repayShares (amount) for CORE.repay
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation that repayShares < totalDebtShares before calling repay

analysis_questions: What happens when CORE.repay is called with amount-as-shares that exceeds actual debt shares?
For fresh pools with 1:1 ratio, does the bug remain hidden?
Are amtOut calculations also corrupted by using the wrong repayAmt?
validation_requirements: Trace return values from _calculateRepaySize through fillOrder's safeTransferFrom and CORE.repay calls
Test fillOrder on a pool with >0 accrued interest
Verify revert occurs in PosManager.updatePosDebtShares when subtracting more shares than exist

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Any attempt to fill a margin order on a pool with accrued interest will fail because: (1) executor transfers too few borrow tokens (share count instead of amount), (2) repay is called with token amount as shares (exceeds position's actual debt shares), causing underflow revert. All stop-loss orders become non-executable, exposing users to unlimited downside.
prerequisites: Interest accrued on any borrow pool used in margin positions

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All stop-loss and take-profit orders permanently non-executable; users lose downside protection
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users with active margin orders
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to fix the return value ordering

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing integration test for fillOrder with non-trivial debt-to-share ratio

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: N/A — no attacker needed, this is a bug
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Bug manifests as soon as any interest accrues

validation_result.impact_summary: The _calculateRepaySize return values are destructured in swapped order at the call site in _calculateFillOrderInfo, causing fillOrder to use debt shares as token amounts and vice versa. After any interest accrual (share:amount ratio ≠ 1:1), all order fills will revert or produce incorrect economic outcomes.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The function _calculateRepaySize declares `returns (uint repayAmt, uint repayShares)`. Inside the function body, these named returns are assigned correctly: `repayShares = totalDebtShares * _order.collAmt / totalCollAmt` (shares) and `repayAmt = ILendingPool(...).debtShareToAmtCurrent(repayShares)` (token amount). Therefore the return tuple is (position 0 = repayAmt = token amount, position 1 = repayShares = debt shares).

However, at the call site in _calculateFillOrderInfo, the destructuring is: `(repayShares, repayAmt) = _calculateRepaySize(...)`. This maps position 0 (token amount) → local `repayShares` and position 1 (debt shares) → local `repayAmt`. The values are completely swapped.

In fillOrder, this causes:
1. `IERC20(borrToken).safeTransferFrom(msg.sender, address(this), repayAmt)` transfers the shares count (too few tokens when interest > 0)
2. `IInitCore(CORE).repay(marginPos.borrPool, repayShares, order.initPosId)` passes the token amount as shares (too many shares)
3. `amtOut` is computed using the wrong `repayAmt`, producing incorrect amounts for the order recipient

When repay's internal `_repay` function calls `safeTransferFrom(hook_address, pool, amtToRepay)`, the hook has insufficient tokens (it received shares-worth instead of amount-worth), causing a revert.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The evidence is unambiguous. The _calculateRepaySize function signature declares `returns (uint repayAmt, uint repayShares)` with position 0 = amount and position 1 = shares. The call site `(repayShares, repayAmt) = _calculateRepaySize(...)` assigns position 0 to repayShares and position 1 to repayAmt, which is the exact opposite of what was returned.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function returns (repayAmt, repayShares) where repayAmt is the token amount and repayShares is the debt share count. Position 0 = amount, Position 1 = shares.

The destructuring `(repayShares, repayAmt) = _calculateRepaySize(...)` assigns position 0 (which is repayAmt/token amount) to the local variable repayShares, and position 1 (which is repayShares/debt shares) to the local variable repayAmt. This swaps amounts and shares.

fillOrder uses the swapped values: repayAmt (actually shares, too low) for token transfer and repayShares (actually token amount, too high) for repay call. The executor provides too few borrow tokens, and the repay tries to use too many shares, causing revert when the hook lacks sufficient tokens for _repay's safeTransferFrom.

calling_sequence: 1. fillOrder loads order and marginPos from storage
4. _calculateRepaySize returns (position0=tokenAmount, position1=debtShares) but caller assigns position0→repayShares, position1→repayAmt
5. _calculateFillOrderInfo computes amtOut using swapped repayAmt (shares instead of amount) — amtOut is wrong
7. IERC20(borrToken).safeTransferFrom(executor, hook, repayAmt=actualShares) — too few tokens transferred to hook
8. IInitCore(CORE).repay(pool, repayShares=actualAmount, posId) — passes token amount as shares to repay
9. In InitCore._repay: sharesToRepay = min(actualAmount, posDebtShares), amtToRepay = debtShareToAmt(sharesToRepay)
10. safeTransferFrom(hook, pool, amtToRepay) REVERTS because hook only has actualShares tokens, not amtToRepay tokens
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability is at the exact point where _calculateRepaySize returns values that are destructured in reverse order. _calculateRepaySize returns (repayAmt=tokenAmount at position 0, repayShares=debtShares at position 1). The call site (repayShares, repayAmt) = _calculateRepaySize(...) maps position 0→repayShares and position 1→repayAmt, swapping them. After interest accrues, debtShareToAmt ratio exceeds 1:1, making tokenAmount > debtShares. This means repayShares (now holding tokenAmount) > actual debt shares, and repayAmt (now holding debtShares) < actual token amount needed. The executor transfers insufficient tokens, and the repay call fails because the hook cannot cover the required repayment amount.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. This is a code logic bug independent of configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an exploit requiring an attacker — it is a code defect that prevents normal functionality. Any attempt to call fillOrder on a pool where interest has accrued (totalDebt/totalDebtShares ratio > 1) will fail. For fresh pools with 1:1 ratio, shares == amounts so the swap is invisible, but as soon as any interest accrues (which happens continuously in any active pool), the bug manifests. All stop-loss and take-profit orders become unfillable, denying users their risk management protections.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL: All margin trading orders (stop-loss and take-profit) are broken after any interest accrual. This means: (1) Stop-loss orders cannot execute, exposing users to unlimited downside risk. (2) Take-profit orders cannot execute, preventing users from realizing gains. (3) The amtOut calculation is also wrong (uses shares instead of amount for repayAmt), so even if the revert didn't occur, the economic outcomes would be incorrect. (4) This affects ALL users with active margin orders across ALL pools where interest has accrued. (5) Requires a contract upgrade to fix.
recommended_mitigations: Fix the destructuring in _calculateFillOrderInfo to match _calculateRepaySize's return order: change `(repayShares, repayAmt) = _calculateRepaySize(...)` to `(repayAmt, repayShares) = _calculateRepaySize(...)`
Alternatively, swap the return declaration in _calculateRepaySize to `returns (uint repayShares, uint repayAmt)` and update the function body accordingly
Add comprehensive integration tests for fillOrder with non-trivial debt-to-share ratios (i.e., after interest accrual)
Consider using a struct for the return type to prevent positional mismatches
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy the full system with a lending pool and margin trading hook. 2. Create a margin position with collateral and debt. 3. Create a stop-loss or take-profit order. 4. Advance time to accrue interest (making shares ≠ amounts). 5. Attempt to call fillOrder — observe the revert. 6. Verify that with 1:1 ratio (no interest), fillOrder succeeds (masking the bug). 7. Apply the fix and verify fillOrder works correctly after interest accrual.
uncertainties: For very newly created pools with minimal interest accrual, the difference between shares and amounts may be negligible, potentially allowing some fills to succeed with rounding
additional_testing_needed: Integration test for fillOrder with significant interest accrual
Fuzz test with varying debt-to-share ratios
Verify amtOut calculation correctness after the fix is applied
